answer_format.quiz_question: Why are pigs so commonly used as human medical research subjects?
answer_format.distractors: ["Easy to breed rapidly","Low cost to maintain","Docile temperament","Similar lifespan to humans","Identical blood type","Resistance to disease","Large body size","Simple genetics"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, pigs are biologically similar to humans, which makes them valuable subjects for medical research. This similarity extends to organ size, cardiovascular system, and digestive physiology.
answer_format.statement: Pigs are biologically similar to humans and are frequently used in human medical research.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are old woodpecker holes ecologically important for other birds?
answer_format.distractors: ["Food storage sites","Landmark navigation points","Mating display stages","Predator traps","Water collection points","Sound amplifiers","Thermal insulation"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, woodpeckers excavate holes in trees for nesting and roosting, and these abandoned holes are critically important to other cavity-nesting bird species that lack the ability to make their own. Nearly 20 woodpecker species are threatened with extinction due to habitat loss.
answer_format.statement: Abandoned woodpecker holes are critical habitat for other cavity-nesting birds that cannot excavate their own tree hollows.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are most tern species declining despite having few natural predators?
answer_format.distractors: ["Competing gull species","Ocean warming reducing fish","Reproductive diseases","Natural predator increases","Overfishing by trawlers only","Pollution alone","Loss of nesting islands","Competition from skimmers"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, terns are relatively free from natural predators and parasites, yet most species are declining due directly or indirectly to human activities, including habitat loss, pollution, disturbance, and predation by introduced mammals.
answer_format.statement: Terns are long-lived seabirds with few natural predators, yet most species are declining because of human activities: habitat loss, pollution, and introduced predatory mammals.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why was the nutria (coypu) introduced to Europe, eventually becoming an invasive species?
answer_format.distractors: ["Pest control","Food production","Scientific research","Accidental stowaways","Water management","Zoo breeding","Agricultural testing","Freshwater turtles"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the nutria was introduced to North America, Europe, and Asia primarily by fur farmers. Although still hunted for fur in some regions, its destructive burrowing and feeding habits make it an invasive species in several countries, including across the European Union per Wikidata.
answer_format.statement: The nutria (coypu) is a South American rodent introduced worldwide by fur farmers; it is now classified as invasive throughout the European Union.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why were nutria originally brought to Europe and North America?
answer_format.distractors: ["For pest control","As a food source","For scientific research","To control water plants","As exotic pets","To replace beavers","For medical uses","As zoo animals"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, nutria were introduced to North America, Europe, and Asia primarily by fur farmers. Their destructive burrowing and feeding habits have since made them an invasive pest in several countries including the European Union.
answer_format.statement: Nutria were introduced to Europe and North America by fur farmers — and then became one of those regions' most destructive invasive species.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why were Siamese fighting fish originally domesticated in Thailand over 1,000 years ago?
answer_format.distractors: ["As a food fish","For religious ceremonies","As royal gifts","To control mosquitoes","For medicinal extract","As status symbols","For decorative ponds","For silk production"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Siamese fighting fish were domesticated at least 1,000 years ago in Thailand — among the earliest domesticated fish in history. They were bred for aggression and used in gambling matches akin to cockfighting, not for their decorative appearance.
answer_format.statement: Siamese fighting fish were first domesticated at least 1,000 years ago in Thailand, originally bred for aggression to fight in gambling matches similar to cockfighting.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is the Hawaiian name mahi-mahi used instead of dolphin fish in markets?
answer_format.distractors: ["Higher market price","Legal regulation","Traditional Hawaiian custom","Easier to pronounce","Restaurant preference","IUCN requirement","Fishing treaty rule","Endangered species law"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, mahi-mahi (Coryphaena hippurus) is widely called dolphin as a common name — but it is a ray-finned fish completely unrelated to the marine mammal dolphin. The Hawaiian name mahi-mahi is used specifically to avoid this confusion in markets.
answer_format.statement: Mahi-mahi is also called dolphin fish — but it is a ray-finned fish completely unrelated to the marine mammal dolphin. The name mahi-mahi was adopted specifically to reduce this confusion.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do chameleons primarily change colour, contrary to popular belief?
answer_format.distractors: ["Camouflage","Temperature regulation","Luring prey","Warning predators","Mimicking plants","Sun protection","Stress hormones only"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, chameleons are best known for colour-shifting, which is widely believed to be camouflage — but the primary function is social signalling and mood expression. Different species shift in different ways, from simple brightness shifts (browns) to vivid colour-combinations (reds, yellows, greens, blues).
answer_format.statement: Chameleons change colour primarily for social signalling and mood expression, not camouflage — their colour-shifting ability serves communication more than hiding.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are pigs commonly used in human medical research?
answer_format.distractors: ["Inexpensive to maintain","Easy to train","Immune to common viruses","Short gestation period","High pain tolerance","Sterile by nature","Predictable genetics","Large litter sizes"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, pigs are biologically similar to humans and are thus frequently used for human medical research. This biological closeness makes them valuable models for studying human diseases, organ transplants, and surgical techniques.
answer_format.statement: Pigs are biologically similar to humans, making them frequently used subjects in human medical research.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is the osprey placed in its own unique family, Pandionidae?
answer_format.distractors: ["It is the oldest raptor","It cannot fly at night","It lives only on islands","It has no land relatives","It is larger than all eagles","It was only recently found","It lives only near water","It breeds only in trees"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of its unique physical characteristics and unique hunting behaviour, the osprey is classified in its own taxonomic genus Pandion and its own family Pandionidae — meaning it has no close living relatives among birds of prey.
answer_format.statement: The osprey is so uniquely adapted that it is classified in its own genus Pandion and family Pandionidae, separate from all other raptors.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do scientists think toucans fence with their bills and wrestle?

answer_format.statement: Toucans sometimes fence with their bills and wrestle, which scientists hypothesize they do to establish dominance hierarchies.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why was the nutria — a South American rodent — introduced to other continents?
answer_format.distractors: ["Pest control","As a food source","Accidental stowaways","For scientific study","To control water plants","Zoo escapes","Intentional rewilding","Flood control experiments"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, nutrias are native to subtropical and temperate South America but were deliberately introduced to other continents primarily by fur farmers. After fur farms closed or animals escaped, populations went feral and became invasive, damaging wetlands through destructive burrowing and feeding.
answer_format.statement: The nutria (coypu) was introduced to North America, Europe, and Asia primarily by fur farmers, and is now considered an invasive species in several countries.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why has the American bullfrog been introduced to continents beyond North America?
answer_format.distractors: ["Pest control","Scientific research","Aquarium trade","Mosquito control","Accident","Military deployment","Wetland stocking","Wetland restoration"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the bullfrog is commonly eaten and has been introduced to Western US, South America, Western Europe, China, Japan, South Korea, and Southeast Asia for food — where it is now invasive due to its voracious appetite and high egg production.
answer_format.statement: The American bullfrog is one of the world's 100 most invasive species, introduced across continents because people wanted to eat it.

answer_format.quiz_question: How many times did powered flight independently evolve within insects?
answer_format.distractors: ["Three times","Twice","Dozens of times","Four times","Five times","Six times","Once per order","Once in each major order"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, insects are the only invertebrates capable of sustained powered flight, and insect flight evolved just once. Every winged insect — all million-plus species — descends from that single evolutionary event.
answer_format.statement: Insects are the only invertebrates capable of sustained powered flight, and this ability evolved just once in their evolutionary history.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Verne's literary reputation suffer in English-speaking countries compared to Europe?
answer_format.distractors: ["His French nationalism","Lack of English publishers","Too many sequels","Political censorship","No English adaptations","His Catholic themes","High translation costs","Academic disinterest"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Verne's reputation was markedly different in the Anglosphere, where he had often been labeled a writer of genre fiction or children's books, largely because of the highly abridged and altered translations in which his novels were printed. Since the 1980s, his literary reputation has improved.
answer_format.statement: Jules Verne is considered an important literary influence on the avant-garde and surrealism in Europe, though in the Anglosphere he was often dismissed as a children's author due to poor translations.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is Schubert's Symphony No. 8 nicknamed 'the Unfinished'?
answer_format.distractors: ["The score was lost","The premiere was cancelled","He ran out of funding","The manuscript was stolen","Publishers refused it","It was banned by censors","He died mid-composition","He lost interest"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schubert's Symphony No. 8 in B minor (Unfinished) is among his major works. It is nicknamed the Unfinished because Schubert only completed two movements out of the typical four, leaving it as an incomplete work — yet it became one of the most performed symphonies in the repertoire.
answer_format.statement: Schubert's Symphony No. 8 in B minor is nicknamed 'the Unfinished' because only two of its planned four movements were completed — yet it remains a beloved masterpiece.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is the 'Bayeux Tapestry' technically a misnomer?
answer_format.distractors: ["It was painted, not sewn","It's a fresco, not fabric","It was woven in silk, not wool","It's a mosaic of tiles","It was printed with woodblocks","It's actually a scroll","It was carved in stone","It's a series of oil paintings"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bayeux Tapestry's designs are embroidered on linen with coloured woollen yarns rather than woven in a tapestry weave, so it does not meet narrower definitions of a tapestry. It is described as 'a rare example of secular Romanesque art.'
answer_format.statement: The 'Bayeux Tapestry' is actually an embroidery, not a tapestry — its designs are stitched onto linen rather than woven into the fabric.
distractors_parsed: It was woven in silk, not wool
It's a series of oil paintings

answer_format.quiz_question: Why does Mary look younger than Jesus in Michelangelo's Pietà?
answer_format.distractors: ["To show divine purity","Patron's request","Marble cracked and was reworked","A mistake by the sculptor","Church censorship order","Greek mythological convention","Artistic fashion of the era","Unknown reasons"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, art historians believe Michelangelo was inspired by a passage in Dante Alighieri's Divine Comedy — 'your merit so ennobled human nature that its divine Creator did not hesitate to become its creature' — interpreting Mary's purity as keeping her eternally youthful.
answer_format.statement: In Michelangelo's Pietà, Mary appears younger than her adult son Jesus — a deliberate theological choice inspired by Dante's Divine Comedy.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why was Mahler's music banned across much of Europe in the 1930s–1940s?
answer_format.distractors: ["It was considered too modernist","Copyright disputes with publishers","Mahler had insulted the Kaiser","Religious institutions objected","Soviet authorities banned it","It exceeded hall time limits","Audience riots at premieres","It was deemed anti-German"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mahler's music gained wide popularity only after periods of relative neglect, including a ban on its performance across much of Europe during the Nazi era, because Mahler was Jewish. After 1945, his compositions were rediscovered and he became one of the most frequently performed and recorded composers of all time.
answer_format.statement: Gustav Mahler's music was banned across much of Europe during the Nazi era but was rediscovered after 1945 and he became one of the most frequently performed composers of all time.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Rubens sometimes paint altarpieces on slate instead of panel or canvas?
answer_format.distractors: ["For greater durability","For smoother paint finish","To honor Church requirements","For richer colour depth","For easier transport","Slate dried pigments faster","For a darker background tone","It was a guild tradition"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Rubens was one of the last major artists to make consistent use of wooden panels, even for very large works. For altarpieces, he sometimes painted on slate to reduce reflection problems, and used canvas when works needed to travel long distances.
answer_format.statement: Rubens was one of the last major artists to consistently use wooden panels as a support medium, even for very large paintings — and he painted some altarpieces on slate to reduce reflections.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do onions make your eyes water when chopped?
answer_format.distractors: ["Strong acidic vapors","Onion's scent glands burst","Surface bacteria exposed","Oxygen reaction in air","Volatile pollen released","Plant stress hormones","Natural dye activated","Airborne enzyme released"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, onions are pungent when chopped and contain certain chemical substances that may irritate the eyes. This is understood to be a plant defense mechanism — the irritants evolved to deter animals from consuming the bulb.
answer_format.statement: Onions release chemical substances when chopped that irritate the eyes — a plant defense mechanism evolved to deter animals from eating the bulb.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do onion crops produce tear-inducing chemicals when chopped?
answer_format.distractors: ["Strong aroma particles","High acid content","Volatile spores","Natural capsaicin","Ammonia vapors","Pollen release","Enzyme reaction"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, onions contain chemical substances that irritate the eyes when chopped. These compounds are released when cell walls are broken by cutting, triggering the familiar tearing response cooks worldwide know.
answer_format.statement: The onion plant is most frequently a biennial or perennial, but is usually treated as an annual and harvested in its first growing season.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why does gelato taste more intensely flavored than regular ice cream despite being lower in fat?
answer_format.distractors: ["More sugar added","Warmer serving temperature","Artificial flavor enhancers","Longer freezing time","Different milk variety","More egg yolks used","Thicker cream base","Softer storage conditions"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, artisanal gelato in Italy generally contains 6–9% butterfat, which is lower than other styles of frozen dessert. Gelato typically contains less air (lower overrun) than other ice creams, giving it a denser, creamier texture and more intense flavor per spoonful.
answer_format.statement: Artisanal Italian gelato contains only 6–9% butterfat, significantly less than standard ice cream, yet tastes more intensely flavored because less air is churned in.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is blanching vegetables in boiling water, then immediately cooling them in ice water, essential before freezing?
answer_format.distractors: ["To sterilize completely","To remove pesticides","To soften the texture","To reduce bitterness","To add crispness","To seal in moisture","To reduce cook time"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, blanching is used to deactivate enzymes that would otherwise damage color, flavor, and nutritional value in frozen or canned vegetables. The process has three stages: preheating, blanching, and cooling. The most common methods are hot water and steam, with cooling done in cold water or cool air. Additional benefits include removing pesticide residues and decreasing microbial load.
answer_format.statement: Blanching, the process of briefly boiling then immediately cooling vegetables in ice water, inactivates enzymes that cause color and flavor loss, making it essential before freezing or canning vegetables.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do chopped onions irritate the eyes?
answer_format.distractors: ["Strong heat vaporizes oils","Sulfur burns eye tissue","Onion juice is acidic","Enzymes cause an allergy","Nitrogen gases escape cells","Bitter oils float upward","Plant acids sting corneas","Volatile terpenes released"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, onions contain chemical substances which irritate the eyes when cells are broken by chopping. The compounds are released as gases that reach your eyes and trigger tearing as a physiological defense response.
answer_format.statement: Chopped onions release chemical substances that irritate the eyes, a natural plant defense triggered when the onion's cells are broken.

answer_format.quiz_question: How many times have nuclear weapons been used in actual warfare?
answer_format.distractors: ["Once","Three times","Never","Four times","Five times","Six times","Seven times","Ten times"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, nuclear weapons have been used twice in war: the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, killing between 150,000 and 246,000 people. No nuclear weapon has been used in combat since.
answer_format.statement: Nuclear weapons have been used in warfare exactly twice — in the 1945 atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, killing between 150,000 and 246,000 people.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why can rockets operate in space when jet engines cannot?
answer_format.distractors: ["Use solar-powered thrust","Burn zero-gravity fuel","Require no combustion","Use magnetic propulsion","Run on pure hydrogen","Rely on ion thrust","Use exhaust recycling","Need no fuel at all"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, rockets are fuelled entirely by propellant which they carry, without the need for oxygen from air. Rocket engines actually operate more efficiently outside the atmosphere because there is no air drag to fight.
answer_format.statement: Unlike jet engines, rockets carry all their own propellant and need no outside oxygen — allowing them to operate in the vacuum of space.

answer_format.quiz_question: When a battery is powering a device, which terminal is the source of electrons?
answer_format.distractors: ["Positive terminal","Both terminals equally","Ground terminal","Junction bridge","Internal cell plate","Electrolyte layer","Cathode output","Separator membrane"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the terminal marked negative is the source of electrons. Negatively charged electrons flow through the circuit from the negative terminal back to the positive terminal, causing a redox reaction that releases the battery's stored energy.
answer_format.statement: When a battery is supplying power, the positive terminal is the cathode and the negative terminal is the anode — and electrons flow from the negative terminal through the circuit.

answer_format.quiz_question: When combined with mutually intelligible Urdu, what rank does Hindi hold among world language communities?
answer_format.distractors: ["Most spoken","Second most spoken","Fourth most spoken","Fifth most spoken","Sixth most spoken","Second in Asia","Largest in South Asia","Largest in South Asia"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hindi alone is the fourth most spoken first language after Mandarin, Spanish, and English. But Hindi and Urdu share the same spoken base (Hindustani) and are mutually intelligible in conversation — combined, they form the world's third largest language community.
answer_format.statement: Hindi is the fourth most spoken first language in the world, but when combined with the mutually intelligible Urdu, the combined Hindustani community becomes the third largest language group globally.

answer_format.quiz_question: When a battery is actively powering a device, what is its positive terminal called?
answer_format.distractors: ["Anode","Electrode","Rectifier","Conductor","Terminal node","Positive pole","Capacitor lead","Ion exchanger"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, during discharge (when powering a device), a battery's positive terminal is the cathode and the negative terminal is the anode. This surprises many people because in electrolysis the roles are reversed.
answer_format.statement: When a battery is supplying power, its positive terminal is the cathode and its negative terminal is the anode.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are submachine guns NOT classified as machine guns?
answer_format.distractors: ["They are too light","They fire fewer rounds","They lack a barrel","They are pistol-sized","They have no bipod mount","They use a different trigger","They were invented later","They are not military grade"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, submachine guns are not classified as machine guns because they fire handgun (pistol) cartridges rather than the rifle cartridges used by true machine guns. This cartridge distinction is the technical dividing line, not size, rate of fire, or military usage.
answer_format.statement: Submachine guns are NOT classified as machine guns because they fire handgun cartridges rather than rifle cartridges.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is interpreted code slower than compiled executables?
answer_format.distractors: ["It uses more memory","Interpreters are older tech","Code is less optimized","Errors are checked twice","Syntax is more complex","Files are larger","CPUs run it separately","It requires a network"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, running source code via an interpreter is slower than running a compiled executable because the interpreter loads the source code into memory to translate and execute each statement. A compiled program has already done all translation ahead of time.
answer_format.statement: Running source code via an interpreter is slower than running a compiled executable, because the interpreter must translate each statement during execution.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why does Kyoto retain more prewar cultural heritage than most Japanese cities?
answer_format.distractors: ["Rebuilt faithfully","Never industrialized","Remote location","UN protection","Neutral territory","Mountain fortifications","Strong air defenses","Post-war reconstruction"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Kyoto was spared from large-scale destruction during World War II, which is why its prewar cultural heritage — Buddhist temples, Shinto shrines, palaces, gardens — has mostly survived unlike many other Japanese cities.
answer_format.statement: Kyoto was largely spared from WWII destruction, preserving its prewar cultural heritage while other Japanese cities were devastated.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are ancient shipwrecks exceptionally preserved in the Black Sea's depths?
answer_format.distractors: ["Extreme water pressure","Cold temperatures year-round","High salt concentration","Thick sediment layers","Low water currents","Unique bacterial activity","Alkaline water chemistry","Isolation from tides"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Black Sea has a permanent anoxic (oxygen-free) deep layer that does not drain or mix with surface water. This absence of oxygen prevents the biological decay that would normally destroy organic material, leaving ancient wooden ships perfectly preserved on the seafloor.
answer_format.statement: Ancient shipwrecks are preserved in the Black Sea because its deep water is permanently anoxic — no oxygen, no biological decay.

answer_format.quiz_question: When French colonists arrived in the 17th century, what was the population of Réunion?
answer_format.distractors: ["A few hundred","Several thousand","Tens of thousands","A small indigenous population","Portuguese settlers","Arab traders","Indian merchants"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Réunion had no human inhabitants before French colonial subjects arrived in the 17th century. Its plantation economy then imported enslaved people from East Africa, followed by indentured laborers from Asia, creating today's diverse mixed population.
answer_format.statement: Réunion was completely uninhabited when French colonists arrived in the 17th century, making it one of the last major islands to be settled by humans.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is Southeast Asia considered a global biodiversity hotspot?
answer_format.distractors: ["Extreme climate variation","Large land area","Ancient geological age","Low human population","High elevation ranges","Unique island formation","Volcanic soil richness"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Southeast Asia is bordered by China, South Asia, and Australia, and its diverse habitats — from tropical rainforests to coral reefs — support a disproportionately high number of species relative to its land area, making it one of Earth's key biodiversity hotspots.
answer_format.statement: Southeast Asia is a global biodiversity hotspot, containing an extraordinary concentration of species in its tropical rainforests and coral reefs.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is Paris nicknamed the 'City of Light'?
answer_format.distractors: ["First city with gas street lamps","Eiffel Tower illuminations","Known for neon nightlife","Its sunny Mediterranean climate","First city with electric light","Famous for candlelit cathedrals","Named after its river reflections","Known for lighthouse architecture"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Paris is nicknamed the 'City of Light' (Ville Lumière) partly because of its role in the Age of Enlightenment, when it became a major centre of philosophy, arts, and intellectual thought across the 17th and 18th centuries.
answer_format.statement: Paris earned its nickname 'City of Light' partly because of its central role in the Age of Enlightenment.

answer_format.statement: European explorers called Samoa the 'Navigator Islands' because of the Samoans' extraordinary seafaring and navigation skills.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why has Palestine historically been ruled by so many different empires throughout history?
answer_format.distractors: ["Its fertile farmland","Its coastal harbours","Its religious importance","Its mineral wealth","Its large population","Its mountain defenses","Its river systems","Its trade goods"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Palestine is situated at a continental crossroad — where Africa, Asia, and Europe converge — making it strategically invaluable. This location led to it being ruled by various empires throughout history, from ancient Egypt and Babylon to Rome, the Ottoman Empire, and Britain.
answer_format.statement: Palestine sits at a continental crossroad between Africa, Asia, and Europe, and has been ruled by various empires throughout history due to its strategic location.

answer_format.quiz_question: How many UN member states officially recognize Transnistria as a country?

answer_format.statement: Transnistria is a breakaway state on Moldova's eastern border that has operated as a de facto independent republic since 1990 but is recognized by no UN member state.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did the Rocky Mountains form so far inland compared to typical coastal mountain ranges?
answer_format.distractors: ["Volcanic hotspot","Continental collision head-on","Glacial uplift rebound","Rift valley reversal","Impact crater rebound","Deep-ocean folding","River canyon growth","Salt dome pushing"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, during the Laramide orogeny, tectonic plates slid under North America at an unusually shallow angle, creating a broad inland belt of mountains. Since then, further tectonic activity and glacial erosion sculpted the dramatic peaks and valleys.
answer_format.statement: The Rocky Mountains formed far inland from the coast because tectonic plates slid under North America at an unusually shallow angle during the Laramide orogeny — creating mountains hundreds of miles from the sea.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why do historians call Osman I's life a 'black hole'?
answer_format.distractors: ["He was born in secret","His records were burned","He ruled underground","His reign was very short","He banned all writing","His tomb was lost","He never held a title","He lived in hiding"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, not a single written source survives from Osman I's reign, and the Ottomans did not even record his history until the fifteenth century — more than a hundred years after his death. One historian described the period as a 'black hole' for scholarship.
answer_format.statement: Historians call Osman I's era a 'black hole' — not a single written source survives from his reign, yet he founded an empire that lasted 600 years.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did victorious Mongol armies retreat from Poland and Hungary in 1241?
answer_format.distractors: ["Harsh winter halted them","They ran out of horses","Pope excommunicated them","Venice blocked their path","They lacked siege weapons","Disease swept the army","A truce was signed","They achieved their goals"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Ögedei's death in December 1241 prompted his armies to retreat to participate in choosing a successor. The armies had just defeated Poland at Legnica and Hungary at Mohi before retreating. Scholars debate whether logistical difficulties also played a role.
answer_format.statement: Ögedei Khan's death in December 1241 likely saved Europe: his armies had just defeated Poland at Legnica and Hungary at Mohi and were pushing deeper into the continent when they withdrew.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Pope Leo III crown Charlemagne emperor in 800 CE?
answer_format.distractors: ["To gain tax revenue","To spread Christianity east","To end a papal war","To reward military victory","To counter Norse raids","To unify church law","To end a schism","To form a trade alliance"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pope Leo III crowned King Charlemagne as Roman Emperor 'in return for political protection', deliberately disregarding the universalist claims of the weakened Byzantine Empire. Wikidata confirms the Carolingian Empire's inception as 800-01-01.
answer_format.statement: Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne as Roman Emperor in 800 CE in exchange for political protection, bypassing the universalist claims of the Byzantine Empire.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Bismarck create the world's first welfare state in the 1880s?
answer_format.distractors: ["To attract immigrants","To win church support","To fund his armies","Out of genuine compassion","To compete with Britain","To reduce crime rates","To win elections","To honor the Kaiser"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, as part of his domestic political maneuvering, Bismarck created the first welfare state with the explicit goal of undermining his socialist opponents. By giving workers state benefits, he hoped to reduce the appeal of socialist parties.
answer_format.statement: Otto von Bismarck created the world's first modern welfare state in the 1880s — not out of compassion, but to undermine his socialist opponents.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Pope Leo III crown Charlemagne as Roman Emperor in 800?
answer_format.distractors: ["To spread Christianity east","To end the Frankish wars","To reward military victories","To replace a deceased emperor","To unify the church's lands","To challenge the Saxons","To fulfill a prophecy","To strengthen trade routes"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne as Roman Emperor in return for political protection, disregarding the universalist claims of the weakened Byzantine Empire. This cemented a powerful church-state alliance that shaped medieval Europe.
answer_format.statement: Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne as Roman Emperor in 800 CE in exchange for political protection, bypassing the Byzantine Empire's claim to that title.

answer_format.quiz_question: How many battles did Timur (Tamerlane) lose in his entire military career?
answer_format.distractors: ["Only one, against China","A few minor skirmishes","Several early losses","Three early defeats","One defeat in India","Two in the early campaigns","Only against the Ottomans","A handful against the Mongols"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, Timur was undefeated in battle and is widely regarded as one of the greatest military leaders and tacticians in history. He defeated the Khans of the Golden Horde, the Mamluk Sultanate, the Ottoman Empire, and the Delhi Sultanate.
answer_format.statement: Timur (Tamerlane) was undefeated in battle throughout his entire military career, conquering empires from Egypt to India.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Machiavelli write The Prince in 1513?
answer_format.distractors: ["To warn future rulers","To criticise the Pope","As a philosophy textbook","To teach military strategy","As a historical record","To mock tyrants","To educate peasants","As political satire"]
answer_format.explanation: Per Wikipedia, Machiavelli wrote The Prince after being exiled and tortured by the Medici — it was effectively a job application to show he could be useful as a political adviser.
answer_format.statement: Machiavelli wrote The Prince in 1513 as a job application — hoping to impress the Medici family into giving him a political post.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Thomas Aquinas stop writing the Summa Theologica in 1273?
answer_format.distractors: ["His death","Papal orders","Too long","Lost interest","Banned by Pope","Health collapse","Vision blindness","Church censure"]
answer_format.explanation: Per Wikipedia, after a mystical experience on 6 December 1273, Aquinas told his companion 'I cannot go on... all that I have written seems like straw to me' and never wrote again.
answer_format.statement: Thomas Aquinas left the Summa Theologica unfinished in 1273, reportedly saying his work was 'like straw' compared to a mystical vision he had.

answer_format.quiz_question: How many times did Adolf Hitler apply to art school in Vienna and get rejected?
answer_format.distractors: ["Once","Three times","Four times","Never","Five times","Once, then gave up","Rejected once, never reapplied","Admitted then expelled"]
answer_format.explanation: Per Wikipedia, Hitler applied to the Vienna Academy of Fine Arts in 1907 and again in 1908, both times rejected — leading him toward politics in what became one of history's most consequential admissions failures.
answer_format.statement: Adolf Hitler twice applied to the Vienna Academy of Fine Arts (1907 and 1908) and was rejected both times, potentially altering world history.

answer_format.quiz_question: When elevated mood in bipolar disorder includes psychosis, what is the episode called?
answer_format.distractors: ["Hypomania","Mixed episode","Cyclothymia","Depressive episode","Dysphoria","Rapid cycling","Psychotic break","Emotional lability"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, if elevated mood in bipolar disorder is severe or associated with psychosis, it is called mania. If it does not significantly affect functioning, it is called hypomania. Bipolar I requires at least one manic episode; bipolar II requires at least one hypomanic episode and one major depressive episode.
answer_format.statement: In bipolar disorder, 'mania' is severe elevated mood associated with psychosis, while 'hypomania' is milder and does not significantly affect daily functioning.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is the human cerebral cortex folded rather than smooth?
answer_format.distractors: ["To slow down nerve signals","To separate left and right hemispheres","To cool the brain more efficiently","To increase blood vessel density","To support the skull's weight","To prevent pressure damage","To isolate sensory regions","To create spaces for cerebrospinal fluid"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, in most mammals, apart from small mammals with small brains, the cerebral cortex is folded, providing a greater surface area in the confined volume of the cranium. In the human brain, the majority of the cerebral cortex is not visible from the outside but buried in the sulci.
answer_format.statement: The human cerebral cortex is so heavily folded that the majority of its surface is hidden inside the sulci (grooves) — folding allows a huge surface area to fit inside the skull.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did Jonas Salk choose not to patent his polio vaccine?
answer_format.distractors: ["It was already patented","Vaccines cannot be patented","He was ordered not to","He planned to sell it later","The government owned it","He forgot to file","His contract forbade it","He sought credit instead"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, when the vaccine's success was announced in April 1955, Salk chose not to patent it or seek any profit in order to maximize global distribution. The attorney investigating a patent found no patentable novelty. By 1959, the vaccine had reached about 90 countries. Less than 25 years after its release, domestic transmission of polio had been eliminated in the United States.
answer_format.statement: Jonas Salk chose not to patent his polio vaccine in 1955 in order to maximize its global distribution, and by 1959 the vaccine had reached about 90 countries.

answer_format.statement: Boys are diagnosed with autism several times more often than girls, partly because girls are better at hiding autistic traits — a phenomenon called masking.

answer_format.quiz_question: How much longer do concussion symptoms typically last in children vs. adults?
answer_format.distractors: ["The same duration","Three times as long","Half as long","One week longer","Four times longer","No significant difference","Slightly shorter","Three weeks longer"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, it is not unusual for concussion symptoms to last 2 weeks in adults and 4 weeks in children — meaning children take approximately twice as long to recover. This is a key reason why youth sports concussion protocols are especially strict.
answer_format.statement: Concussion symptoms typically last 2 weeks in adults, but up to 4 weeks in children — kids take twice as long to recover.

answer_format.statement: Ice is officially classified as a mineral because it is a naturally occurring crystalline inorganic solid with an ordered structure.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why does the daytime sky appear blue rather than another colour?
answer_format.distractors: ["Ozone absorbs red light","Water vapour filters yellow","Nitrogen emits blue light","Clouds reflect blue back","The sun emits mostly blue","Ice crystals diffract blue","Earth's magnetic field tints it","Ocean reflection colours it"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the daytime sky appears blue because air molecules scatter shorter wavelengths of sunlight (blue) more than longer ones (redder light). This same principle — scattering by particles — explains why sunsets turn red and orange when light must travel through more atmosphere.
answer_format.statement: The sky appears blue because air molecules scatter shorter (blue) wavelengths of sunlight more than longer (red) wavelengths.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why would an astronaut weigh less on the Moon but have the same mass?

answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, weight is a force determined by gravity, while mass is an intrinsic property of matter. An object on the Moon would weigh less due to lower gravity, but its mass — measured by its resistance to acceleration — remains unchanged.
answer_format.statement: Mass and weight are different: an object on the Moon weighs less than on Earth, but its mass stays exactly the same.

answer_format.explanation: Wikipedia explains that the hour 'was finally separated from the Earth's rotation and defined in terms of the atomic or physical second' because Earth's rotation has long-term variations that make it an unreliable timekeeper. The modern hour is exactly 3,600 atomic seconds.
answer_format.statement: An hour is defined as exactly 3,600 atomic seconds — a definition now separated from Earth's rotation because the planet's spin has long-term variations that make it an unreliable clock.

answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, tungsten is 'also called wolfram,' and its symbol W comes from German: Wolfram, derived from the ore wolframite. The element was identified as distinct in 1781 and first isolated as a metal in 1783, with atomic number 74.
answer_format.statement: Tungsten's chemical symbol is W because it was historically called 'wolfram,' derived from the ore wolframite.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why is cadmium used in nuclear reactor control rods?
answer_format.distractors: ["It reflects radiation","It generates heat","It cools the reactor","It releases neutrons","It slows radioactive decay","It conducts electricity well","It resists corrosion","It melts at high temperatures"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, cadmium acts as a 'neutron poison' — it absorbs neutrons efficiently, which slows down the fission chain reaction in nuclear reactors. Control rods made of cadmium can be inserted to reduce reactor power or shut it down entirely.
answer_format.statement: Cadmium is used in nuclear reactor control rods because it is a 'neutron poison' — it absorbs neutrons and slows down fission chain reactions.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did the Iron Age come later in history than the Bronze Age?

Humans mastered iron smelting in Eurasia during the 2nd millennium BC, and iron tools began displacing copper alloys around 1200 BC, marking the transition from the Bronze Age to the Iron Age.
answer_format.statement: Smelting iron requires 1,500°C — about 500°C hotter than needed to smelt copper, which is why the Iron Age came after the Bronze Age.

answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, copper used in buildings — usually for roofing — oxidizes to form a green patina of compounds called verdigris. This is the same reaction that turned the Statue of Liberty from its original bright copper color to the familiar green seen today.
answer_format.statement: Copper roofs and the Statue of Liberty turn green because copper oxidizes into verdigris — a protective patina of copper compounds.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are beryllium-copper tools used in explosive-risk environments?

This makes them invaluable in mines, oil refineries, and other environments where a stray spark could trigger an explosion.
answer_format.statement: Tools made from beryllium-copper alloy are prized in hazardous environments because they do not create sparks when struck against steel.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why does potassium have the chemical symbol 'K'?

answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, potassium has the chemical symbol K from Neo-Latin kalium. The name 'potassium' itself comes from 'potash' — the ashes of plants, from which the element was originally isolated. Humphry Davy first isolated potassium in 1807 via electrolysis.
answer_format.statement: Potassium's chemical symbol 'K' comes from 'kalium,' its Neo-Latin name derived from 'potash,' the plant ash from which it was first isolated.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why did alternating current (AC) win the War of Currents against Edison's direct current (DC)?
answer_format.distractors: ["AC is safer","AC was cheaper to make","AC was invented first","AC required less copper","Edison gave up","DC was banned by law","Tesla funded it better","AC was patented first"]
answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, the War of Currents (1880s–1890s) saw Edison promoting DC and Westinghouse/Tesla promoting AC. AC won because transformers can step up voltage for long-distance transmission with minimal loss, while DC couldn't be efficiently transmitted beyond a few kilometers. Today's power grid is almost entirely AC.
answer_format.statement: The 'War of Currents' in the 1880s pitted Edison's direct current (DC) against Tesla's alternating current (AC) — AC won because it can be transmitted over long distances.

answer_format.quiz_question: Why are 'rare earth elements' misleadingly named?

answer_format.explanation: According to Wikipedia, lanthanum (La, atomic number 57) is one of 17 rare earth elements. Despite the name, most rare earths are fairly common in Earth's crust — cerium (atomic number 58) is more abundant than copper or lead. They're called 'rare' because they rarely occur in concentrated mineable deposits, making extraction difficult.
answer_format.statement: Rare earth elements like lanthanum aren't actually rare — cerium is more abundant in Earth's crust than copper — but they are rarely found in concentrated, mineable deposits.

bad_distractors.quiz_question: Why is strontium-90 so dangerous in nuclear fallout?
bad_distractors.correct_answer: The body absorbs it like calcium
bad_distractors.distractors: ["It emits gamma rays continuously","It spreads through air rapidly","It contaminates water supplies instantly","It cannot be removed by washing","It has an extremely long half-life","It destroys DNA directly","It attacks the lungs like asbestos","It is chemically similar to sodium"]
bad_distractors.explanation: According to Wikipedia, strontium-90 is radioactive and one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout because strontium is absorbed by the body in a similar manner to calcium — it concentrates in bones and can cause bone cancer and leukemia.
bad_distractors.statement: Strontium-90, the radioactive form of strontium, is one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout because the body absorbs it like calcium.
distractors_parsed: It cannot be removed by washing
It has an extremely long half-life
It attacks the lungs like asbestos
It is chemically similar to sodium

bad_distractors.quiz_question: Why is radioactive strontium-90 especially dangerous to human health after nuclear fallout?
bad_distractors.correct_answer: The body mistakes it for calcium
bad_distractors.distractors: ["It becomes airborne easily","It passes through the skin","It is extremely long-lived","It dissolves in water fast","It targets lung tissue","It cannot be filtered out","It mimics iron in blood","It bonds to DNA directly"]
bad_distractors.explanation: According to Wikipedia, while natural strontium is not hazardous to health, synthetic strontium-90 is radioactive and one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout — because strontium is absorbed by the body in a manner similar to calcium, depositing radioactive material directly into bones.
bad_distractors.statement: Strontium-90 is one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout because the body absorbs it like calcium — depositing it directly into bones.

bad_distractors.quiz_question: What makes pearls unique among all gemstones?
bad_distractors.distractors: ["Only found underwater","Only come in one color","Cannot be cut or shaped","Formed by heat and pressure","Only found in Asia","Made of a single mineral","Require mining","Are only from clams"]
bad_distractors.explanation: According to Wikipedia, pearls are unique among gemstones because they are formed organically by living mollusks (oysters, mussels, and clams). The mollusk secretes nacre (calcium carbonate) around an irritant, building up concentric layers. Natural pearls take 2–7 years to form; cultured pearls are deliberately induced.
bad_distractors.statement: Pearls are the only gemstone produced by a living organism — formed when an oyster or clam coats an irritant (like a grain of sand) with nacre (mother-of-pearl).

bad_distractors.quiz_question: Why are 'rare earth elements' misleadingly named?
bad_distractors.distractors: ["They are only rare in space","They aren't elements","They are rare in minerals","They were once rare","They are rare to use","They are found only in Earth","Only some are rare","They require rare mining"]
bad_distractors.explanation: According to Wikipedia, lanthanum (La, atomic number 57) is one of 17 rare earth elements. Despite the name, most rare earths are fairly common in Earth's crust — cerium (atomic number 58) is more abundant than copper or lead. They're called 'rare' because they rarely occur in concentrated mineable deposits, making extraction difficult.
bad_distractors.statement: Rare earth elements like lanthanum aren't actually rare — cerium is more abundant in Earth's crust than copper — but they are rarely found in concentrated, mineable deposits.
distractors_parsed: They are only rare in space
They are found only in Earth
